title: The Dangers of the US-Iran Conflict
source: The Guardian - The Guardian Weekly 13 March 2026-1
content:
While an evaluation of previous US conflicts in the Middle East reveals many similarities, the current war against Iran may prove to be the most dangerous and consequential yet. This is the third Gulf War and the umpteenth outbreak of conflict since the United States took over as the dominant power in the Middle East at the end of the Cold War. It is arguably the most dangerous, consequential, and confused of them all. The destruction and chaos spreading across the region confirm the Middle East’s status as the world’s pre-eminent crisis factory. It also raises questions as to how US presidents declare they are ending US interference, only to be lured back in.

Since the Second World War, the US has set out to oust a government in the Middle East on average once a decade. On almost every occasion, it has left the country, and the US, worse off as unexpected consequences eventually emerge. As the US embarks on yet another regime change—this time in Iran, a country of 90 million people—the sense of foreboding is profound. Already the timelines are extending, and the sense is growing that the leadership is gambling with the fate of a country about which it knows next to nothing.

The first Gulf War, in 1990-91, at least had the advantage of being of a containable scope, purpose, and duration. Once Saddam Hussein invaded Kuwait in a warped blow for pan-Arabism, the US pushed the Iraqi leader’s forces back with relative ease. This was achieved while maintaining a broad supportive Arab coalition, partly by ensuring Israel did not respond to Saddam’s provocations to become involved. However, the current situation presents a much more volatile scenario.
